coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of environmentally-friendly carbon quantum dots for “on-off” rapid fluorescent sensing of folic acid, Fe3+, and Ca2+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Qi
Mao, Hui-Hui
Xue, Mingyue
Feng, Xiao-Zhen
Han, Guo-Cheng
Chen, Zhencheng
Kraatz, Heinz-Bernhard
coredata.dc:description: Detecting abnormal levels of folic acid (FA), Fe3+, and Ca2+ in the human body is crucial due to their association with various diseases. Orange peel carbon quantum dots (OP-CQDs) with blue fluorescence (λex = 370 nm, λem = 440 nm) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method when waste biomass orange peel was utilized as the carbon source. An “on-off” fluorescence sensor, referred to as OP-CQDs-FA-Fe3+/Ca2+, was developed for quantitative analysis of the drug FA, as well as the metal ions Fe3+ and Ca2+. Additionally, the sensor was utilized for fluorescence imaging of HepG2 liver cancer cells, demonstrating its exceptional biocompatibility. The concentration of FA displayed a linear relationship in the range of 5.00–90.00 μmol L−1 and 0.30–2.50 mmol L−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μmol L−1. The spiked recovery in folic acid tablets ranged from 95.70% to 104.50%. The addition of Fe3+ or Ca2+ leads to a reduction or restoration of the fluorescence intensity in the OP-CQDs-FA system, resulting in an “on-off” sensor mode. The spiked recoveries in human serum samples were 100.15%–100.48% for Fe3+ and 98.05%–98.18% for Ca2+. This study highlights the considerable potential of OP-CQDs for ion detection, drug analysis, and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Folic acid
Fe3+ and Ca2+
Fluorescence probe
Pharmaceutical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of OP-CQD and linear detection of FA, Fe3+, and Ca2+ standard solutions
Determination of fluorescence quantum yields of OP-CQDs
Linear detection of FA, Fe3+, Ca2+ standard solutions
Analysis of fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime
Response of OP-CQDs as fluorescent probes for FA detection
Reaction of different concentrations of FA with Fe3+ and Ca2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the financial assistance provided from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61661014 , 81873913 , 61861010 , 61627807 ), the Nature Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2018GXNSFAA281198 , 2018GXNSFBA281135 ), Guangxi One Thousand Young and Middle-aged College and University Backbone Teachers Cultivation Program, Guangxi Human Physiological Information Non Invasive Detection Engineering Technology Research Center, and finally Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Biomedical Sensors and Intelligent Instruments. Financial support from NSERC and from the University of Toronto is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution of OP-CQDs. (B) Particle size distribution. (C) HR-TEM maps of OP-CQDs. (D) XRD pattern of OP-CQDs. (E) FTIR pattern of OP-CQDs. High-resolution spectra of OP-CQDs. (F) XPS full spectrum of OP-CQDs, (G) C1s, (H) O1s, (I) N1s.

(A) UV–vis absorption and best excitation-emission spectra of OP-CQDs (inset: photos of OP-CQDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV (right) irradiation). (B) Emission spectra of OP-CQDs at excitation wavelengths of 320 nm–420 nm. (C) Bright field. (D) 405 nm Excitation fluorescence imaging map. (E) Merged images of (C) and (D).

(A) Plots of fluorescence emission spectra of OP-CQDs in the presence of 0.00–100.00 μmol L−1 concentration of FA and linear plot of the fluorescence intensity enhancement value (ΔF=(F–F0)/F0) of OP-CQDs versus FA concentration. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of OP-CQDs in the presence of 0.10–10.00 mmol L−1 concentration of FA and linear plot of fluorescence intensity burst value (ΔF=(F–F0)/F0) of OP-CQDs versus FA concentration.
Effect of different concentrations of (A) Fe3+, (B) Ca2+ on the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs-FA system.
Synthesized OP-CQDS to detect FA, Fe3+, and Ca2+.
Determination and recovery experiments of FA in folic acid tablets (n = 3).
Detection of Fe3+ and Ca2+ in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of environmentally-friendly carbon quantum dots for “on-off” rapid fluorescent sensing of folic acid, Fe3+, and Ca2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin 541004, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, University of Toronto Scarborough, Toronto, Ontario, M1C 1A4, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical and Environmental Sciences, University of Toronto Scarborough, Toronto, Ontario M1C 1A4, Canada

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to the work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detecting abnormal levels of folic acid (FA), Fe3+, and Ca2+ in the human body is crucial due to their association with various diseases. Orange peel carbon quantum dots (OP-CQDs) with blue fluorescence (λex = 370 nm, λem = 440 nm) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method when waste biomass orange peel was utilized as the carbon source. An “on-off” fluorescence sensor, referred to as OP-CQDs-FA-Fe3+/Ca2+, was developed for quantitative analysis of the drug FA, as well as the metal ions Fe3+ and Ca2+. Additionally, the sensor was utilized for fluorescence imaging of HepG2 liver cancer cells, demonstrating its exceptional biocompatibility. The concentration of FA displayed a linear relationship in the range of 5.00–90.00 μmol L−1 and 0.30–2.50 mmol L−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 μmol L−1. The spiked recovery in folic acid tablets ranged from 95.70% to 104.50%. The addition of Fe3+ or Ca2+ leads to a reduction or restoration of the fluorescence intensity in the OP-CQDs-FA system, resulting in an “on-off” sensor mode. The spiked recoveries in human serum samples were 100.15%–100.48% for Fe3+ and 98.05%–98.18% for Ca2+. This study highlights the considerable potential of OP-CQDs for ion detection, drug analysis, and cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Folic acid (FA), a naturally occurring water-soluble B vitamin, is abundant in various food sources [1]. Its chemical structure is depicted in Fig. S1. However, metabolic disturbances and poor absorption in the body can result in FA deficiency, despite its synthesis by human intestinal bacteria from dietary sources [2]. Such deficiency has been linked to various conditions, including neonatal neural tube abnormalities [3], megaloblastic anemia [4], vascular dementia in the elderly [5], Parkinson's disease [6], depression [7], type II diabetes, and cardiovascular disease [8]. Conversely, excessive FA supplementation can have detrimental effects on infant neurodevelopment [9]. Moreover, it can mask symptoms of vitamin deficiency and interfere with zinc metabolism, among other complications [10]. Therefore, achieving a rapid and sensitive analysis and detection of FA is of utmost importance. As an essential nutrient, Fe3+ plays a crucial role in various biological metabolic processes, including respiration, DNA synthesis, and oxygen transport. Abnormal iron levels in the body can contribute to the development of several diseases, including Parkinson's syndrome, Alzheimer's disease, anemia, and cancer [11]. Ca2+ is involved in various crucial functions in living organisms, including signal transduction, extracellular effects, apoptosis, transcription, muscle contraction, and bone formation. Disruptions in the Ca2+ signaling pathway can lead to neurodegeneration, muscle deficiency, heart disease, and skin disorders [12]. Consequently, there is an imperative to develop sensitive, convenient, and cost-effective methods for the individual detection of FA, Fe3+, and Ca2+.

Detection methods for include liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [13–15], capillary electrophoresis [16], high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) [17,18], electrochemical analysis [19], and fluorescence analysis [20,21]. Our group has developed electrochemical sensors for the detection of FA using CuNPs/CoNPs/GCE [22] and Fc(COOH)/CuNPs/GCE [23]. Fluorescence analysis has gained favor among these detection methods due to its convenient operation, straightforward sample pretreatment process, and ability to provide visual detection results [24,25].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are fluorescent carbon nano-materials that have been extensively studied due to their straightforward preparation, availability of raw materials, excellent water solubility, light stability, and biocompatibility. CQDs possess the general characteristics of nano-materials [26], as well as remarkable photostability and biocompatibility, enabling the identification and detection of targets in complex environments. Compared to other types of fluorescence sensors, CQDs offer the benefits of straightforward preparation, affordability, and a wide range of raw material sources. Unlike traditional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs are free from heavy metal ion doping, exhibit lower environmental and cytotoxic effects, and remain biocompatible even at higher concentrations. Moreover, the surface of CQDs is rich in hydrophilic groups, including carboxyls, amino groups, and hydroxyl groups, which imparts excellent solubility and stability in aqueous solutions. Compared to organic dyes, CQDs exhibit superior photostability. Additionally, their nanometer size allows easy cellular uptake through endocytosis, enabling long-term cell imaging.

CQDs have garnered substantial attention owing to their exceptional optical properties, including enhanced fluorescence intensity, resistance to photobleaching, and adjustable luminescent color. They have extensive applications in the detection of anions, cations, small organic molecules, and macromolecules in research studies.

Quinoline derivatives have been used to modify CQDs for the specific recognition of Zn2+ [27]. N and F co-doped carbon quantum dots (NFCDs) were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method [28]. Given the rapid quenching of fluorescence intensity in NFCDs by CIO− (<1 min) and the linear correlation between fluorescence intensity and CIO− concentration, LOD of 12.00 mmol L−1 allows for the determination of dissolved chloride ions in water samples. Song and co-workers [29] synthesized functional dual-emissive CQDs, referred to as dCQDs (dual-emission function CQDs). In the presence of lysine, the surface of dCQDs undergoes passivation, resulting in a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity at 440 nm with increasing lysine concentration. The fluorescence intensity at 624 nm was quenched with increasing pH. Xue and co-workers [30], prepared natural biomass carbon dots (NBCDs) from lychee exocarp and developed targeted near-infrared fluorescence imaging nanoprobes by loading transferrin (Tf) and a photosensitizer onto NBCDs for the photodynamic therapy (PDT) of tumor cells and mouse tumors.

In this study, orange peel, derived from the waste biomass material of orange (Latin name: Citrus reticulata), was utilized as the carbon source. It was mixed with ultrapure water and subjected to a one-step hydrothermal method for synthesizing fluorescent CQDs. This method is known for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental friendliness. The OP-CQDs underwent characterization using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV–vis), and fluorescence spectroscopy. Furthermore, the applications of OP-CQDs as fluorescence sensors in drug analysis and cell imaging were investigated. OP-CQDs can function as “on-off” fluorescence sensors for quantitatively analyzing of drug FA, as well as the detection of essential metal ions Fe3+ and Ca2+. Additionally, they can be utilized for fluorescence imaging of HepG2 liver cancer cells.

Folic acid (FA) was acquired from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The orange peels utilized in the experiments were obtained from nearby fruit stores. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), DMEM Korose medium, trypsin, and 100 U/Penicillin-Streptomycin were obtained from Sigma, human hepatocellular carcinoma cell line (HepG2) cells were procured from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China), Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Aladdin. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used in the experiments. All other reagents were of analytical grade and utilized without further purification.

The morphological structure and size of the orange peel carbon quantum dots (OP-CQDs) were examined using the TecnaiG2F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips, Holland), and the crystal structures were obtained by analyzing MiniFlex X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku, Japan) patterns, ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Electron, America) was used to determine the chemical composition and relative content, Nicolet iS20 Fourier transforms infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher, America) was used to determine the composition of functional groups on the surface. In addition, UV–Vis (Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy) absorption spectra were recorded on a T9 double-beam UV spectrometer (Beijing Pujie General Instrument Co., Ltd., China), Fluorescence spectra were measured using the FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA, France), and the fluorescence lifetime was obtained using an FLS1000 Steady-state, transient fluorescence spectrometer (Shanghai Huiji Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of OP-CQD and linear detection of FA, Fe3+, and Ca2+ standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh orange peel was washed, dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h, and then ground into a powder. A weight of 0.15 g of orange peel powder was measured and suspended in 25 mL of ultrapure water. The suspension was then placed in an autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling, the mixture was filtered using rapid filter paper to remove unreacted particles and impurities. Subsequently, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm ultrafiltration membrane to obtain a solution of carbon quantum dots (OP-CQDs). The solution was diluted tenfold and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

A mixture containing 20 μL of prepared OP-CQDs and 20 μL of FA solutions with varying concentrations was prepared. The mixture was then combined with 160 μL of PBS (pH 7.0) and mixed using a vortexer. The reaction mixture was allowed to incubate at room temperature for 15 min, and then the fluorescence intensity was measured at excitation wavelength λex = 370 nm, emission wavelength λem = 440 nm, with a 5.0 nm slit width. Additionally, a set of common anionic, amino acid, and biological small molecule substances were used to assess the selectivity of OP-CQDs towards FA. After combining 20 μL of prepared OP-CQDs, 20 μL of the corresponding FA standard solution, and 140 μL of PBS (pH 7.0), the mixture was incubated for 15 min. Subsequently, 20 μL of different concentrations of Fe3+ and Ca2+ were added and mixed using a vortexer. The resulting mixture was allowed to react for an additional 15 min, followed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the mixture under the same conditions. The detection mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fluorescence quantum yields of OP-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of CQDs reflects their fluorescence intensity. FLQY of OP-CQDs was determined by comparing it to the quinine sulfate standard (0.10 mol L−1 of H2SO4) [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear detection of FA, Fe3+, Ca2+ standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared OP-CQDs (20 μL) and FA solutions with varying concentrations (20 μL) were combined with 160 μL of PBS (pH 7.0) and mixed using a vortexer. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 15 min, and the fluorescence intensity was measured at an excitation wavelength (λex) of 370 nm, emission wavelength (λem) of 440 nm, and a slit width of 5.0 nm. Additionally, various common anionic, amino acid, and small biological molecules were employed to assess the selectivity of OP-CQDs towards FA. Subsequently, 20 μL of the prepared OP-CQDs, 20 μL of the corresponding FA standard solution, and 140 μL of PBS (pH 7.0) were incubated for 15 min. Then, 20 μL of different concentrations of Fe3+ and Ca2+ were added, mixed using a vortexer, and incubated for an additional 15 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the resulting mixture was measured under same conditions.

Three folic acid tablets (each containing 0.40 mg) were crushed, and a precisely weighed amount of the powdered equivalent to a single tablet was dissolved in a suitable quantity of 0.10 mol L−1 NaOH. The resulting mixture was sonicated for 10 min and then centrifugation. The supernatant was extracted, filtered, and then transferred to a 5 mL volumetric flask, which was stored at 4 °C under light protection. The solution was subsequently diluted threefold before use in the assay. Spiked recovery experiments were conducted to determine the concentration of FA in the actual samples. A mixture containing 20 μL of the pretreated FA tablet solution, 140 μL of PBS (pH 7.0), and 20 μL of OP-CQDs was prepared. Subsequently, different concentrations of FA standard solutions (0.00, 10.00, 20.00, 40.00 μmol L−1) were added and mixed. The resulting mixture was used for the assay and incubated for 15 min. Human serum samples were subjected to triple filtration and diluted 600-fold with PBS (pH 7.0). The spiked recovery method was employed to determine the concentrations of Fe3+ and Ca2+ in the serum. A mixture containing 120 μL of PBS (pH 7.0), 20 μL of OP-CQDs, and 20 μL of FA standard solutions (100.00 μmol L−1 or 10.00 mmol L−1) was prepared and incubated for 15 min. Subsequently, 20 μL of the serum samples and 20 μL of different concentrations of Fe3+ or Ca2+ standard solutions (0.00, 10.00, 20.00, 40.00 μmol L−1) were added to the mixture and incubated for 15 min for the assay.

The cytotoxicity of OP-CQDs on HepG2 cells was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. HepG2 cells, a human hepatocellular carcinoma cell line, were cultured nearby under sterile conditions on an ultra-clean bench. The cells were maintained in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C. For the experiment, OP-CQDs solutions at varying concentrations (0.00, 50.00, 100.00, 150.00, 200.00, and 250.00 μg mL−1) were added to 96-well plates with HepG2 cells at 80% confluency. The absorbance values at 450 nm were measured using an enzyme marker for each well.

Trypsin-digested HepG2 cells were seeded into a cell culture dish specifically designed for confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and incubated for 24 h. After incubation, OP-CQDs at a concentration of 0.80 mg mL−1 were added and incubated for 12 h. The OP-CQDs solution was rinsed to remove cells that did not take up the nanoparticles, and then the cells were fixed using 4% paraformaldehyde. Cell imaging was performed using CLSM with excitation at a wavelength of 405 nm and acquisition through the blue channel (450–550 nm).

The morphology and structure of OP-CQDs were observed using TEM, as depicted in Fig. 1 A. Statistical analysis using the statistical software ImageJ on 100 OP-CQDs in TEM revealed that OP-CQDs exhibited monodispersity and a uniform spherical distribution. The size statistical analysis of the particles, presented in Fig. 1B, indicated an average particle size of 3.70 nm, demonstrating their nanometer-scale dimensions. Fig. 1C illustrates the lattice spacing of OP-CQDs as 0.227 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene [14]. This finding indicates that OP-CQDs have a graphene-like structure [32].

The lattice structure of the prepared CQDs was determined using XRD analysis. The XRD characterization of solid OP-CQDs, with a scan range set to 10–60° as shown in Fig. 1D, revealed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.87°, indicating a significant degree of disorder in the carbon atoms of the synthesized OP-CQDs. Comparison with the standard card confirmed that the peak corresponds to the (002) face of graphene, consistent with the HR-TEM results, confirming the graphene-like carbon structure of OP-CQDs.

FTIR analysis was performed to analyze the functional groups on the surface of OP-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 1E. The peaks observed at 3406.29 cm−1 and 1604.42 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of OP-CQDs are attributed to O–H/N–H and N–H stretching vibrations [33], respectively. The peak at 2928.29 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C–H bond [34], while the peak at 1699.38 cm−1 indicates the presence of CO [35]. The absorption peaks at 1453.60 cm−1 and 804.04 cm−1 represent C–H bending vibrations, and the bands at 1273.26 cm−1 and 1027.47 cm−1 suggest the presence of C–O groups [36]. These findings indicate that the surface of OP-CQDs contains hydrophilic groups, which contribute to their high dispersibility and stability in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the presence of these functional groups enables highly sensitive identification and detection through mutual binding with target functional groups.

XPS analysis was performed to characterize the elemental composition and bonding of OP-CQDs. Fig. 1F displays the observed peaks of C1s (285.07 eV), O1s (532.31 eV), and a small peak of N1s (399.22 eV), indicating that the sample predominantly comprises carbon (C) and oxygen (O) elements with a minor presence of nitrogen (N) elements. The deconvolution peaks at 284.73 eV, 286.29 eV, and 288.41 eV in Fig. 1G represent C–C/CC bonds, C–O bonds, and O–CO bonds, respectively [37]. Furthermore, the peaks at 531.9 eV and 532.85 eV in Fig. 1H correspond to C–O–C and CO bonds, respectively [38]. In Fig. 1I, the peaks at 399.49 eV and 401.68 eV indicate the presence of amino N and graphite N, respectively [39]. These peaks confirm the abundance of water-soluble functional groups on the surface of OP-CQDs, consistent with the findings of the FTIR characterization.

The optical properties of OP-CQDs were analyzed using a UV–Vis absorption spectrometer and a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The left side lines in Fig. 2 A, labeled as “Abs.”, represent the UV–Vis absorption spectra plots of OP-CQDs, while the right side lines, with λex = 370 nm and λem = 440 nm, depict the optimal excitation and emission fluorescence spectra plots of OP-CQDs. The absorption plot clearly shows a prominent absorption peak at 280 nm for OP-CQDs, attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC double bond and the n-π* transition of the CO double bond [25]. Fluorescence spectroscopy demonstrates that OP-CQDs exhibit a highest emission peak at 440 nm when excited at 370 nm. The inset in the figure provides additional confirmation of the favorable fluorescence properties of the prepared OP-CQDs.

The emission peak of the OP-CQDs exhibited a gradual red shift as the excitation wavelength increased, as observed in Fig. 2B. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity was highest at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, as shown in Fig. S2A. This behavior can be attributed to the abundance of functional groups on the surface of the prepared OP-CQDs, which facilitates energy level transitions between π-π* and results in multiple emission sites. As a result, an emission spectrum that depends on the excitation wavelength is observed. Alternatively, the presence of a large number of surface defects on the OP-CQDs, resulting from inadequate passivation, may also contribute to these observations [40].

The suitability of OP-CQDs for biological applications was evaluated by analyzing their cytotoxicity against the human hepatoma cell line HepG2. The cytotoxicity of OP-CQDs was evaluated, and cell viability was determined using Equation (1).
(1)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
[
O D
(
T r e a t e d
)
/ O D
(
C o n t r o l
)
]
× 100 %
The absorbance value in the presence of OP-CQDs is denoted as OD (Treated), while the absorbance value in their absence is denoted as OD (Control), as shown in Fig. S2B.

The obtained results demonstrate the excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity of OP-CQDs, supporting their potential use as markers for cell labeling and imaging. OP-CQDs were observed to enter the cytoplasm through endocytosis, without adversely affecting the morphology of HepG2 cells (Fig. 2C). This suggests that OP-CQDs can function as non-destructive fluorescent probes for intracellular applications. Moreover, upon excitation by a 405 nm laser, the cells emitted blue fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 2D. The merged images in Fig. 2E confirm the successful internalization of OP-CQDs into the cells, highlighting their outstanding biocompatibility. The results indicate that OP-CQDs possess great potential as optical imaging probes in the field of bioimaging.

The fluorescence intensities of the prepared OP-CQDs were evaluated following 60 min of irradiation under a xenon lamp (Fig. S3A) and 45 min of irradiation under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S3B). The errors in the plots were below 5%, indicating the reliability of the measurements. The graphs demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs remained relatively stable after extended irradiation under a xenon lamp. However, slight decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed after 45 min of irradiation under a 365 nm UV lamp, but it still retained 84.77% of the initial intensity. These results demonstrate the superior photobleaching resistance of the synthesized OP-CQDs, rendering them suitable for applications in photoelectric analysis and cell imaging.

The experiment utilized PBS buffer solutions with pH values ranging from 2.0 to 12.0, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. S4. The fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs was lowest at pH 2.0, while there were no significant differences among the remaining pH values. This phenomenon may be attributed to the susceptibility of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of OP-CQDs to H+ ions, resulting in aggregation and a burst of fluorescence under highly acidic conditions. Conversely, under neutral and alkaline conditions, OP-CQDs undergo deprotonation, leading to monodispersion facilitated by electrostatic repulsion, resulting in high and stable fluorescence intensity [41]. Thus, PBS with pH 7.0 was selected as the optimal buffer solution. However, as depicted in Fig. S4, OP-CQDs can also be utilized for analytical applications in complex pH environments spanning from pH 3.0 to 12.0.

The experiment investigated the salt resistance of OP-CQDs, recognizing that the majority of nanoscale materials are vulnerable to alterations in salt ion concentration in their surrounding environment. Results depicted in Fig. S5A and Fig. S5B demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs remained unaltered when the NaCl concentration ranged from 0.00 to 2.50 mol L−1 and the KCl concentration varied from 0.00 to 1.50 mol L−1, with errors in the plots being below 5%. These findings indicate the satisfactory salt stability of OP-CQDs, providing an advantageous for their utilization in biochemical analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime
ce:section.ce:para:
The FLQY of OP-CQDs is determined using Equation (2).
(2)
Q =
Q R
×
F S
F R
×
η S
2
η R
2
×
A R
A S
in this equation, Q represents the fluorescence quantum yield of the sample, F denotes the measured integrated area of fluorescence intensity, A represents the absorbance, and subscripts R and S correspond to the reference quinine sulfate and sample OP-CQDs, respectively. It should be noted that the ratio of η
S
/η
R = 1. The measured data is presented in Table S1, and the calculated FLQY of OP-CQDs is 28.14%.

Apart from investigating the quantum yields of OP-CQDs, the experiments also involved analyzing their fluorescence lifetimes, as depicted in Fig. S6. The average fluorescence lifetime (
τ
a v e
) was determined using Equation (3).
(3)
τ
a v e
=
(
A 1
×
τ 1 2
+
A 2
×
τ 2 2
)
/
(
A 1
×
τ 1
+
A 2
×
τ 2
)

The average fluorescence lifetime of OP-CQDs at the optimal excitation wavelength is measured to be 3.27 ns. The short fluorescence lifetime suggests rapid recombination of excited charges in OP-CQDs, indicating their strong photostability.

CQDs have found widespread applications in ion detection due to their remarkable fluorescence properties. The selectivity of OP-CQDs for various metal ions was evaluated by exposing target ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+) at a concentration of 10.00 mmol L−1 as target ions to assess their impact on the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs. The detection results are presented in Fig. S7A, with all errors in the figure being less than 5%. By comparing the values of fluorescence intensity ΔF (ΔF = F/F0, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity at λem = 440 nm before and after the addition of target ions, respectively), it was observed that the introduction of other common metal ions had no significant impact on the fluorescence signal of OP-CQDs, except for Cu2+ and Fe3+, which exhibited a minor effect on the fluorescence intensity.

In addition to investigating the response of OP-CQDs to metal ions, it was equally important to assess the influence of common anions on OP-CQDs. This study investigated the impact of common anions, including SO4 2−, SO3 2−, NO3 −, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CO3 2−, and HCO3 − on the fluorescence signal of OP-CQDs. The fluorescence intensity at λem = 440 nm before and after adding target ions (F0 and F, respectively) was used to calculate the response (ΔF = F/F0). Fig. S7B illustrates that the fluorescence signal of OP-CQDs remains unaffected by the seven common anions chosen, with errors in the plots being less than 5%.

To investigate the specificity of OP-CQDs, we introduced equal concentrations (10.00 mmol L−1) of Cys, Glu, Hcy, His, Lys, Phe, Trp, Tyr, GSH, Glucose, Biotin, AA, UA, DA, and FA into the OP-CQDs solution. The results are displayed in Fig. S7C, with (Δ FF/F0 representing the fluorescence intensities at λem = 440 nm before and after the addition of the target substances (F0 and F, respectively), and the errors in the graphs being less than 5%. The figure reveals that, apart from FA significantly affecting the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs, various amino acids and small biological molecules have minimal influence on the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs, thereby confirming the solution's specificity for detecting FA. Building upon these findings, OP-CQDs were employed as quantitative fluorescent sensors to detect FA in the experiment.

The pH and reaction time for FA detection using OP-CQDs were independently optimized in the experiment, with all errors in the figure being less than 5%. Fig. S8A demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of FA, upon reacting with OP-CQDs, remained constant across various pH conditions. Notably, the optimal FA detection results using OP-CQDs were achieved at a pH of 7.0 compared to the control group. In this experiment, the influence of reaction time on the system was examined by analyzing the impact on the fluorescence intensity at different time intervals: 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50 min (ΔF = F/F0). The results, as depicted in Fig. S8B, indicate that a reaction time of 15 min yields the optimal outcome. Consequently, the experiment determined that the most effective buffer solution for FA detection using OP-CQDs was PBS with a pH of 7.0, while the optimal reaction time was determined to be 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of OP-CQDs as fluorescent probes for FA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of FA concentration on the fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs within the range of 0.00–10.00 mmol L−1 was analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer under optimal experimental conditions. The observed trend is presented in Fig. S9. The fluorescence intensity of OP-CQDs exhibited an initial increase followed by a decrease, as depicted in Fig. 3 .

As shown in Fig. 3A, the linear equation describing the relationship between FA concentration within the range of 5.00–90.00 μmol L−1 was determined as ΔF = 0.0045 CFA+5.8505 × 10−4, R1 2 = 0.9987. From Fig. 3B, the linear equation of FA concentration in the range of 0.30–2.50 mmol L−1 was ΔF = 0.337–0.3481 CFA, R2 2 = 0.9994, and the minimum detection limit (LOD) of the sensor was 0.15 μmol L−1 (n = 11). These findings demonstrate that the OP-CQDs fluorescence sensor is capable of detecting FA concentrations within the range of 5.00 μmol L−1-2.50 mmol L−1, exhibiting the advantage of a broad linear detection range. However, the varying fluorescence trends of OP-CQDs at different linear ranges introduce uncertainty when inferring FA concentration solely based on fluorescence intensity. To address this issue, the influences of various common ions and minute biological molecules, including amino acids (10.00 mmol L−1), on the OP-CQDs-FA system were examined under optimal experimental conditions in the presence of 100.00 μmol L−1 and 10.00 mmol L−1 FA concentrations. The corresponding results are presented in Fig. S10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction of different concentrations of FA with Fe3+ and Ca2+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system was reduced by the addition of Fe3+ in the presence of 100.00 μmol L−1 FA, showing an “on-off” sensor mode, in the presence of 10.00 mmol L−1 FA, the fluorescence of the OP-CQDs burst by FA was restored by the addition of Ca2+, showing an “off-on” sensor mode. Crucially, the presence of additional interfering substances did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system. Therefore, when the linear range of FA concentration is unknown, the addition of Fe3+ to the detection system can provide valuable insights. A decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the system indicates a measured FA concentration within the linear range of 5.00–90.00 μmol L−1. Conversely, an unchanged fluorescence intensity suggests a measured FA concentration within the linear range of 0.30–2.50 mmol L−1. Additionally, the inclusion of Ca2+ in the detection system can be used to validate this observation through the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. The development of this “on-off” fluorescence sensor presents a novel approach to address the challenge of uncertain FA concentration. Furthermore, the system exhibits the capability to detect diverse concentrations of Fe3+ and Ca2+.

This experiment analyzed the fluorescence reduction of the system when exposed to 100.00 μmol L−1 FA and the fluorescence enhancement of the system when exposed to 10.00 mmol L−1 FA, by combining the linear detection of FA concentration and the “on-off” detection mechanism. The results are presented in Fig. 4 .

From Fig. 4A, it is evident that the fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system gradually decreases as the concentration of Fe3+ increases in the presence of 100.00 μmol L−1 FA. The relationship is linear within the range of 5.00–80.00 μmol L−1, described by the equation ΔF = 0.9700–0.0033 CFe 3+ (ΔF=F/F0), R3 2 = 0.9983. LOD was 4.91 μmol L−1, as seen in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system gradually enhanced with increasing Ca2+ concentration in the presence of 10.00 mmol L−1 FA, and the linear detection range was the same as that of Fe3+, showing an excellent linear relationship in the range of 5.00–80.00 μmol L−1, with the equation ΔF = 0.0024 CCa 2+ −0.0128 (ΔF=(F–F0)/F0), R4 2 = 0.9981, and LOD of 3.16 μmol L−1. Therefore, OP-CQDs-FA can be used as a fluorescence sensor to detect Fe3+ and Ca2+ concentrations in the range of 5.00–80.00 μmol L−1.

To examine the precise reaction process between OP-CQDs and FA, the UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of OP-CQDs, OP-CQDs-FA, FA, and its photolysis products were experimentally measured, as depicted in Fig. S11 (with CFA = 55.00 μmol L−1 in the linear enhancement of fluorescence (Fig. S11A) and CFA = 2.00 mmol L−1 (Fig.S 11B) as an example, where CFA denotes the concentration of FA).

As shown in Fig. S11A, it can be seen that 55.00 μmol L−1 FA has two absorption peaks at 277 nm and 355 nm. OP-CQDs showed an absorption peak at 280 nm, and when FA was added to OP-CQDs solution, the peak at 280 nm was not displaced, but a new absorption peak at 360 nm appeared, combined with Fig. S11C and Fig. S11D analysis, the possible reason is that the aqueous solution of FA underwent photolysis when the concentration of FA was low probably influenced by the light conditions during the assay [14,16]. The fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system was increased in the range of 0.00–100.00 μmol L−1 due to the reaction of pteridine, a photolysis product, with OP-CQDs. The fluorescence emission of OP-CQDs was enhanced by pteridine through surface passivation, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), and chemical modifications, resulting in the optimization of radiative recombination and reduction of non-radiative decay pathways. Fig. S11B shows that the absorption peak at 280 nm shifted slightly after adding 2.00 mmol L−1 FA to the OP-CQDs solution, possibly due to the binding of FA to OP-CQDs during the reaction. These functional groups will be coupled by hydrogen bonding and produce an aggregation effect, resulting in the shift of the absorption peak of the system and the burst of fluorescence.

In the OP-CQDs-FA system, the fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system was decreased upon the addition of Fe3+ (0.00–100.00 μmol L−1), potentially suggesting complex formation between Fe3+ and functional groups on the surface of CQDs [42]. This complex formation led to the modification of energy levels and electronic structure of CQDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching. After being coordinated with Fe3+, the non-radiative transfer processes from the excited electrons of CQDs to the d orbitals of metal ions caused attenuation of fluorescence [43]. In the OP-CQDs-FA system, the presence of Ca2+ (0.10–10.00 mmol L−1) effectively increased the fluorescence intensity. This can be attributed to the interaction between Ca2+ and the surface functional groups of the OP-CQDs, causing the detachment of FA molecules from the quantum dots. As a result, the fluorescence of the OP-CQDs was restored and intensified. The stronger binding affinity between FA and Ca2+ facilitated the preferential binding of FA to Ca2+ rather than the surface of the OP-CQDs. This displacement was driven by the stronger bonding interaction between FA and Ca2+ compared to FA and the quantum dots. Consequently, the release of FA molecules from the surface of the OP-CQDs allowed for the recovery of the fluorescence signal.

To achieve as-prepared OP-CQDs practical application value, the concentration of FA in folic acid tablets was determined using the spiked recovery method [44–47], and the results are presented in Table 1 . As observed from the table, the actual detected value of the undiluted standard FA solution was 56.82 ± 4.81 μmol L−1, corresponding to a FA content of 0.38 mg. This value exhibited an accuracy of 94.25% when compared to the standard content of folic acid tablets. Moreover, the spiked recovery assay yielded recoveries of FA in folic acid tablets ranging from 95.70% to 104.50%, with RSDs ranging from 0.58% to 4.19%. These results indicate an improved sensitivity and accuracy of the sensor. Furthermore, the p-values obtained from the one-way ANOVA method for significance testing were all <0.05, affirming the statistical significance of the assay. Therefore, the proposed method is well-suited for the accurate detection of FA concentration in real samples.

The concentrations of Fe3+ and Ca2+ in human serum samples were determined by the spiked recovery method. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of Fe3+ and Ca2+ in human serum were 100.15%–100.48% and 98.05%–98.18%, with RSDs of 1.90%–3.30% and 3.00%–4.60%, respectively, indicating that the sensitivity and accuracy of the sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and Ca2+ were elevated. The significance test by one-way ANOVA method the p-values were <0.05, indicating that the OP-CQDs-FA system is statistically significant for detecting Fe3+ and Ca2+ and can be well used for the spiked detection of Fe3+ and Ca2+ in human serum samples.

In this study, blue-fluorescent OP-CQDs were prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal method, employing waste biomass orange peel as a carbon source. The synthesis process was environmentally friendly, with no need for additional agents, and it involved straightforward processing at a low cost. OP-CQDs exhibited an enhanced fluorescence intensity when reacting with the FA photosite Pteridine in the presence of FA concentrations ranging from 0.00 to 100.00 μmol L−1. At FA concentrations ranging from 0.10 to 10.00 mmol L−1, hydrogen bonds were formed between the functional groups (-OH, –COOH, and –NH2) of FA and –OH and –COOH groups of OP-CQDs, leading to aggregation and subsequent fluorescence quenching. OP-CQDs demonstrated remarkable sensitivity and selectivity, with a LOD of 0.15 μmol L−1, enabling the detection of FA in folic acid tablets. At the same time, the establishment of the OP-CQDs-FA system realized the rapid “on-off” detection of Fe3+ and Ca2+ in human serum samples, the fluorescence intensity of the OP-CQDs-FA system was reduced by the addition of Fe3+ in the presence of 0.10 mmol L−1 FA, showing an “on-off” sensor mode, in the presence of 10.00 mmol L−1 FA, the fluorescence of the OP-CQDs burst by FA was restored by the addition of Ca2+, showing an “off-on” sensor mode. Because Fe3+ interacts with the functional groups on the surface of OP-CQD to form complexes. Thus electrons transferred when OP-CQD was excited, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity on the surface of OP-CQD. After adding Ca2+, the binding ability of FA to Ca2+ was more vital than that of hydrogen bonding with OP-CQDs, and the fluorescence intensity increased. OP-CQDs were found to be biocompatible by HepG2 cell imaging. These results demonstrate the great potential of OP-CQD in ion detection, drug analysis, and cell imaging.

Qi Zhao: Investigation; Validation; design; Writing – original draft. Hui-Hui Mao: Investigation; Validation; design. Mingyue Xue: Investigation; Validation; design. Xiao-Zhen Feng: Investigation; Validation. Guo-Cheng Han: Conceptualization; Writing – review & editing; Supervision. Zhencheng Chen: Supervision; review. Heinz-Bernhard Kraatz: Supervision; review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial assistance provided from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61661014, 81873913, 61861010, 61627807), the Nature Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2018GXNSFAA281198, 2018GXNSFBA281135), Guangxi One Thousand Young and Middle-aged College and University Backbone Teachers Cultivation Program, Guangxi Human Physiological Information Non Invasive Detection Engineering Technology Research Center, and finally Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Biomedical Sensors and Intelligent Instruments. Financial support from NSERC and from the University of Toronto is gratefully acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensor for ultrasensitive sensing of biotin based on heme conjugated with gold nanoparticles and its electrooxidation mechanism
Food Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qi Zhao is a postgraduate of Biomedical Engineering in School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology. His research interests are developing fluorescent nanoprobes and biomolecules detection.

Hui-Hui Mao is a postgraduate of Biomedical Engineering in School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology. Her research interests are developing fluorescent nanoprobes and biomolecules detection.
Mingyue Xue is currently a professor of Chemistry at Lingnan Normal University. Her research interests focus on synthesis of nanomaterials, cell imaging and biosensor applications.

Xiao-Zhen Feng is currently an associate professor of analytical chemistry at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. Her research interests focus on electrochemical biosensors and surface enhanced Raman scattering.

Guo-Cheng Han received his Ph.D. degree in applied chemistry from the Central South University (2011). He is currently a professor of Biomedical Engineering at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. His research interests cover synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in biosensors and electrochemical analysis.

Zhencheng Chen received his Ph.D. degree in Biomedical Engineering from the Xi'an Jiaotong University (2002). He is currently a professor of Biomedical Engineering at Guilin University of Electronic Technology. His research interests include biosensors and electrical devices.

Heinz-Bernhard Kraatz received his Ph.D. in 1993 from the University of Calgary and carried out postdoctoral work at the University of Maryland. In 1998, he joined the Department of Chemistry at The University of Saskatchewan and was appointed full professor in 2007. In 2011, he accepted a professorship at the University of Toronto. His research interests cover organometallic chemistry, electrochemical biosensor and electrochemical analysis.
